preFix.title: Green birch coat pegs
preFix.subtitle: A set of three traditional bifurcated coat pegs from birch branches
preFix.excerpt: Three coat pegs cut from birch branches where a side branch forks from the main stem. The fork forms the natural hook. Each peg is trimmed and mounted on a backing board. The whole set takes one afternoon.
preFix.sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for the natural-fork treen method; Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) for the hallway coat-peg arrangement.

content.text: The bifurcated coat peg uses the natural fork of a branch where a side shoot diverges from the main stem. The fork is the hook; the main stem is trimmed to a mounting plate. Three or four pegs from the same birch branch produce a matched set.
Green
birch trims and carves cleanly with a sloyd knife; the pegs are dried before mounting.
Wood
Birch is the recommendation: the bark is attractive and can be left on for visual effect; the wood is strong enough for a laden coat hook; it dries without checking when the section is small. Each
blank

is a fork section where the main stem is 25 to 35 mm in diameter and the side branch is 15 to 20 mm. The main stem is cut 100 mm below the fork and 50 mm above; the side branch is left 80 to 100 mm long and trimmed to a smooth peg end. Hazel forks are an alternative with a warmer bark colour.
Tools
Bow saw: for cutting the fork sections from the branch.
Sloyd knife: for trimming the peg end, the mount flat, and any side knots.
Cordless drill: for the mounting screw hole and the pilot hole in the backing board.
Leather strop.
Danish oil: for sealing the dried birch.
Before you start cutting.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts (blade away from body) and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool is more dangerous than a sharp one. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. Apply firm pressure, elevate, and dress. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
Selecting and cutting the forks

Look for forks where the branch diverges at 30 to 50 degrees from the main stem. A 30-degree fork makes a flatter peg, good for lighter items; a 50-degree fork makes a steeper hook that holds a heavy coat more securely. Cut the main stem section 100 mm below the fork with the bow saw. Cut the main stem 50 mm above the fork. Trim the side branch peg to 90 mm from the fork with the saw.
Trimming

Use push cuts with the sloyd knife to remove any side knobs or small side shoots. Round the peg tip to a smooth dome; a sharp cut peg end may snag fabric. Flatten the back of the main stem section (the part that will sit against the wall) with light push cuts. The flat only needs to be 20 to 25 mm wide; it positions the peg against the backing board and wall.
Drying

Air-dry the three fork pegs for two to three weeks at room temperature. They may check slightly at the end-grain faces; small checks at the sawn ends are normal and do not affect strength. Do not drill the mounting holes while the wood is green; drill into dried wood for a stable hole.
Finishing and mounting

Apply one coat of Danish oil, wipe off after ten minutes, and allow to dry for four hours. Drill a 4 mm pilot hole through the flat back of each peg for the mounting screw. Countersink the screw hole slightly so the screw head sits flush. Mount all three on a backing board of 18 mm hardwood at 150 mm spacing, or mount individually on the wall.
Variations
Double fork: where a branch divides into two from the main stem, the double fork holds two items on separate pegs from a single wall mount.
Bark on: leave the birch bark intact for a rustic look. The outer bark peels slowly over a few years indoors, which adds to the natural character of the piece.
Hazel version: hazel bark has a warm brown colour that contrasts well on a white wall.
Care
Wipe occasionally with a dry cloth. A very light re-oil every year or two keeps the wood from drying out under the dryness of a centrally-heated hallway.
Troubleshooter

attrs.fix: Forks are stress points and small cracks here are common. A crack that does not cross the load-bearing part of the peg (the section where a coat hangs) does not affect function. Seal the crack with a drop of Danish oil.
attrs.cause: Green wood stress at the fork junction released on drying
attrs.symptom: The peg cracked at the fork during drying

attrs.fix: Use at least a 50 mm screw into a stud, or a wall anchor rated for 10 kg minimum. A riven birch peg with a full coat weighs less than 3 kg; the wall fixing is the limiting factor, not the wood.
attrs.cause: Mounting screw too short or not driven into a stud
attrs.symptom: The peg pulls away from the wall under a laden coat

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
